question: Why does Fastify use JSON Schema for validation instead of runtime libraries like Zod?

options.text: JSON Schema is compiled into optimized code at startup -- validation and serialization are many times faster than runtime checks
JSON Schema is the only standard that supports TypeScript
options.explanation: TypeScript isn't tied to JSON Schema. Zod, Joi, and other libraries work perfectly with TS. JSON Schema's advantage is compilation into fast code via Ajv/fast-json-stringify

options.text: Zod and Joi can't validate nested objects
options.explanation: Zod and Joi handle nested objects perfectly. Fastify chose JSON Schema for performance: Ajv compiles schemas into native JS code

options.text: JSON Schema is simpler to write than Zod
options.explanation: JSON Schema is often more verbose. The advantage is performance: Ajv compiles the schema once at startup, and subsequent validation runs as optimized if/else code

question: What happens when a Fastify plugin is wrapped in fastify-plugin (fp)?

options.text: The plugin breaks encapsulation -- its decorators and hooks become available in the parent context and sibling plugins
The plugin loads before all others
options.explanation: fastify-plugin doesn't affect load order. It removes encapsulation -- decorators (e.g., request.db) become visible outside the plugin

options.text: The plugin gets access to Fastify's private API
options.explanation: fp doesn't grant private API access. It removes the scope boundary so decorators and hooks are available in parent and sibling contexts

options.text: The plugin becomes a singleton and loads only once
options.explanation: Plugins already load once. fp breaks encapsulation -- without it, each register() creates an isolated context

question: Which Fastify lifecycle hook is best for performing authorization (access control checks)?

options.text: preHandler -- request body is parsed, validation passed, permissions can be checked before business logic
onRequest -- the earliest hook, check immediately
options.explanation: onRequest is good for authentication (token verification) but not authorization. In onRequest, the body isn't parsed and params aren't validated yet -- there may not be enough data for permission checks

options.text: preSerialization -- check before sending the response
options.explanation: preSerialization is too late -- business logic has already executed. Authorization should be checked BEFORE the handler to avoid unnecessary work

options.text: onSend -- last chance to block the response
options.explanation: onSend is even later -- the response is already serialized. Authorization should be in preHandler, before business logic execution

question: How does response schema in Fastify protect against leaking sensitive data?

options.text: fast-json-stringify serializes only fields specified in response schema -- password_hash and other internal fields are automatically dropped
Fastify automatically detects fields named password and removes them
options.explanation: Fastify doesn't analyze field names. The response schema determines which fields appear in the response -- everything else is dropped during serialization

options.text: Response schema encrypts sensitive fields before sending
options.explanation: Schema doesn't encrypt data -- it defines the response structure. Fields not specified in the schema are simply not included in the JSON response

options.text: Fastify throws an error if there are extra fields in the response
options.explanation: Fastify doesn't throw an error -- it silently drops fields not specified in response schema. This is safe default behavior
